Applied to Brain Tumor MRI Detection

Time Frame
Section 1 — Motivation

The Problem We're Solving

Brain MRI scan — bright mass (centre-right) is a tumour. Source: Darabi, 2026.

  • MRI scans encode biological structures as proton density variations — tumours appear as bright regions against surrounding tissue
  • Visual identification is unreliable: edges lack defined borders due to noise and low-contrast gradients between healthy and pathological tissue
  • Using a Brain Tumor MRI dataset, this project evaluates and compares various edge detection techniques on a set of brain MRI scans to determine the most robust algorithm to isolate and locate tumours
  • Key challenge: a balance must be found to maintain localisation accuracy of subtle edges without loss of precision
Why automation? While a medical practitioner visually analyses MRI scans for tumour diagnosis, researchers also employ automated anomaly detection techniques based on classical edge detection methods — offering reproducibility and speed at scale.
The Story We're Telling

60 Years of Solving One Problem

1963
Roberts Cross
Forward Difference · O(h)
No smoothing → noise destroys results
1968–70
Sobel / Prewitt
Central Difference · O(h²)
Smoothing is heuristic, not principled
1980
Marr–Hildreth
LoG · Zero-crossings
Recall = 0.982, Precision = 0.183
1986
Canny
Calculus of Variations
Mathematically optimal — NMS hurts on MRI
1987
Deriche
IIR · Padé Approximant
Canny quality, O(1) per pixel
1998–2000
Sub-Pixel · DWT
Interpolation · Multiresolution
Precision or scale — pick your trade-off

Each step is a numerical methods concept: Taylor error → regularization → variational optimization → rational approximation → multiresolution

Section 1 — Problem Statement

Why MRI Edge Detection?

  • MRI images are 2D grayscale matrices where biological structures are encoded as varying proton densities
  • Tumors are hard to spot: edges lack defined borders due to noise and low-contrast gradients. Differentiation is highly sensitive to noise η
  • Goal: a balance must be found to maintain localisation accuracy of subtle edges without loss of precision
  • Clinical relevance: while a medical practitioner visually analyses MRI scans for tumour diagnosis, researchers also employ automated anomaly detection techniques based on classical edge detection methods
  • Mathematical model: f(i,j) = I(i·h, j·h) + η(i,j) — estimate ∇I from discrete noisy samples to isolate healthy vs. pathological tissue boundaries
Image as Sampled Function
f(i, j) = I(i·h, j·h) + η(i, j)
Gradient Magnitude — Edge Strength
|∇I| = √( (∂I/∂x)² + (∂I/∂y)² )
Evaluation Metrics
Precision = TP/(TP+FP) · Recall = TP/(TP+FN)
F1 = 2·P·R / (P+R)
Section 3 — Methodology

Evaluation Pipeline

50 MRI Scans
YOLO polygon labels → binary edge masks
→
Run Detector
9 algorithms at swept threshold
→
Edge Masking (ROI)
Crop to tumour bounding box ± 5 px using an ROI mask
→
Distance Matching
Pixel distance to ground truth ≤ 2 px → TP
→
P / R / F1
Averaged across 50 scans
Matching rule We computed the pixel-wise distance between detected edges and the ground truth mask using OpenCV's distance transform. A detected pixel is counted as a True Positive if it lies within 2 pixels of any ground truth edge — avoiding unfair penalties for sub-pixel localisation differences.
Section 4 — Mathematical Preliminaries

Finite Difference Approximations

Taylor expansion underpins every edge operator. The choice of scheme determines truncation error.

Forward Difference
f′(x) ≈ [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h
Error: O(h) · Roberts
Central Difference
f′(x) ≈ [f(x+h) − f(x−h)] / 2h
Error: O(h²) · Sobel, Scharr
Second Derivative
f″(x) ≈ [f(x−h) − 2f(x) + f(x+h)] / h²
Error: O(h²) · Marr–Hildreth
Convolution — The Unifying Operation
(K ∗ f)(i, j) = Σₚ Σ_q K(p, q) · f(i − p, j − q)

Every finite difference stencil applied to an image is a discrete convolution with kernel K. The optimal threshold T = 40 was confirmed experimentally across all first-order operators.

Section 4 — Mathematical Preliminaries

Math Concepts & Their Role in Edge Detection

Math Concept Role in Edge Detection
Taylor Series Justification for finite difference weights — every kernel is a truncated Taylor expansion
Separability Optimization for real-time performance — a 2D convolution splits into two 1D passes, reducing O(n²k²) to O(n²k)
Vector Calculus Combining x and y partial derivatives into gradient magnitude |∇I| and direction θ = atan2(∂I/∂y, ∂I/∂x)
Laplacian Finding the exact centre of an edge via zero-crossings of ∇²I — used by Marr–Hildreth LoG
Associativity of Convolution Combining smoothing and differentiation into one step: ∇(G ∗ I) = (∇G) ∗ I — the basis of the Canny and LoG pipelines
Section 5.1 — First-Order Methods

Roberts, Prewitt, Sobel & Scharr: Finite Differences

Roberts · 1963 · O(h)
Roberts Cross
+1
0
0
−1
0
+1
−1
0

Forward diagonal difference. T = 40 optimal.

Sobel · 1968 · O(h²)
Sobel Operator
-1
0
+1
-2
0
+2
-1
0
+1

B-spline [1,2,1] smoothing. T = 40 optimal.

Scharr · 2000 — Least-Squares [3,10,3]

Minimises angular error in Fourier domain. Near-perfect rotational isotropy. Also T = 40 optimal — all three first-order operators tie at F1 = 0.556.

Roberts Cross · T = 40 optimal · F1 = 0.556
Sobel · T = 40 optimal · F1 = 0.556
Scharr · T = 45 optimal · F1 = 0.556
Pros
  • Highest F1 score in suite (0.556) — ties for 1st place
  • High recall (0.763) — captures broad tumour boundaries
  • Computationally simple, O(n²), easy to implement
  • Scharr achieves near-perfect angular isotropy
Cons
  • No integrated smoothing — sensitive to scanner noise η
  • Roberts O(h) truncation error — coarser approximation
  • Fixed single scale — cannot distinguish fine vs. coarse edges
  • Moderate precision (0.458) — some false positives on noise
Section 5.2 — Discrete Wavelet Transform

Haar DWT: Multiresolution Analysis

  • 2D Haar transform splits the image into LL (approximation), LH (horizontal), HL (vertical), HH (diagonal noise)
  • Adaptive threshold λ = kσ̂√(2 log N), where σ̂ = median(|HH|) / 0.6745
  • Optimal k = 2.5 selected by F1 on training subset. Resulting in F1 = 0.499, Recall = 0.761
  • Analyses edges simultaneously at fine, medium, and coarse resolution
Staircase artefact Diagonal frequency separation creates stepwise approximation of diagonal edges — precision drops to 0.412 vs 0.458 for Sobel
Haar DWT · Sensitivity k Sweep · k = 2.5 optimal
Pros
  • Multi-scale — analyses all spatial frequencies simultaneously
  • Adaptive threshold calibrated to per-image noise floor
  • High recall (0.761) — matches first-order operators
  • Robust MAD noise estimator — not thrown off by outlier edges
Cons
  • Staircase artefact on diagonal edges — precision penalty
  • Lower precision (0.412) than Sobel (0.458)
  • Haar basis cannot represent diagonals continuously
  • Overall F1 = 0.499, below first-order operators
Section 5.3 — Variational Optimization

Canny Detector: Optimal by Design

Canny's Optimality Criteria (1986)
SNR = |∫h| / √(∫h²)    Loc = |h′(0)| / √(∫h′²)
  • Calculus of variations proves the optimal filter = first derivative of a Gaussian
  • Non-Maximum Suppression(NMS) thins edges to 1 px — ideal for sharp edges, suppresses diffuse MRI boundaries
  • σ = 0.1 optimal — hysteresis ratio T_high/T_low = 3 held fixed
  • Recall drops to 0.577 vs 0.763 for Sobel due to NMS on diffuse gradients
Canny · σ Sweep · σ = 0.1 optimal · F1 = 0.487
Pros
  • Mathematically proven optimal among linear filters
  • Single-pixel-wide edges — high spatial precision
  • Hysteresis links weak fragments, reduces false positives
  • Best precision among non-sub-pixel methods (0.458)
Cons
  • NMS suppresses diffuse MRI tumour boundaries — recall = 0.577
  • σ = 1.0 blurs fine anatomical detail before detection
  • Lower F1 (0.487) than simple operators — optimal ≠ best here
  • Requires manual σ selection per dataset
Section 5.4 — Second-Order Methods

Marr–Hildreth LoG: Zero-Crossing Detection

5-Point Laplacian Stencil
L = [0,1,0 / 1,−4,1 / 0,1,0]  ·  LoG = ∇²(Gσ ∗ I)
  • TThe image is first convolved with a Gaussian kernel (parameterised by σ) to suppress additive noise, before any differentiation is applied
  • Edges at zero-crossings of ∇²(G∗f) — inflection points in intensity
  • Linearity allows the Laplacian to be applied directly to the Gaussian kernel prior to image convolution: LoG(x,y) = [∇²G(x,y)] ∗ I(x,y)
  • Zero-crossings are only accepted if the absolute difference between sign-inverted neighbours exceeds a sensitivity threshold, providing robustness against residual noise
  • σ = 0.7 optimal, yielding the best balance between noise suppression and edge localisation
  • Most extreme result: Recall = 0.982, Precision = 0.183, F1 = 0.305
LoG (Second-Order) · σ Sweep · σ = 0.7 optimal
Pros
  • Gaussian smoothing prior to differentiation effectively reduces high-frequency noise artifacts.
  • Robust across various orientations and scales; zero-crossings naturally produce closed contours, ideal for delineating tumour regions.
  • Achieves the highest recall of all tested methods — Recall 0.982 — finding nearly every true edge.
Cons
  • Lowest precision (0.183) — fires on every noise fluctuation
  • Lowest F1 score (0.305) — last place in the suite
  • Zero-crossing criterion cannot distinguish true edges from noise residuals
  • Large σ merges nearby boundaries — scale sensitivity
Section 5.5 — Deriche Edge Detector

Deriche Edge Detector

FINAL OUTPUT — BIDIRECTIONAL RECURSIVE FILTER
L(n) = y₁(n) + y₂(n)
  • Replaces Gaussian smoothing with an infinite impulse response (IIR) filter for quicker computation.
  • Bidirectional recursive pass ensures zero-phase shift; coefficients governed by scaling parameter α
  • α swept over {0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5} — α = 0.8 identified as optimal
  • F1 = 0.388, Precision = 0.306, Recall = 0.573
Deriche · α Sweep · α = 0.8 optimal · F1 = 0.388
Pros
  • High accuracy and robustness to noise — significant edges are not missed
  • Uses all previous sample points for a more precise smoothing effect
  • O(1) per pixel regardless of σ — computationally efficient
Cons
  • Can create slight disruptions in edge amplitude
  • Inefficient for blurred or soft edges
  • Lower F1 (0.388) relative to other methods in the suite
Section 5.6 — Sub-Pixel Methods

Sub-Pixel Methods: Interpolation & Curve Fitting

Polynomial Sub-Pixel · T = 0.5 optimal · F1 = 0.476
Quadratic Interpolation
x* = (g₋₁ − g₁) / 2(g₋₁ − 2g₀ + g₁)

Fits parabola g(x) = ax² + bx + c to three gradient samples g₋₁, g₀, g₁. Displacement x* ∈ (−0.5, 0.5) is added to the integer coordinate. Reduces quantization error from O(h) to O(h²).

Lindeberg Scale-Space · T = 0.5 optimal · F1 = 0.434
Scale-Space Edge Condition
Lx²Lxx + 2LxLyLxy + Ly²Lyy = 0

Zero-crossing of second-order directional derivative in gradient direction. Third-order finite difference stencils with 5-pixel support and O(h²) truncation error.

Pros
  • Highest precision in the suite — Poly: 0.502, Lindeberg: 0.504
  • Sub-pixel accuracy — best for measuring boundary position
  • Lindeberg adapts scale automatically to local structure, robust across varying feature sizes.
Cons
  • Sacrifices recall — Poly: 0.528, Lindeberg: 0.441
  • Polynomial method degrades in noisy or blurred regions where gradient profile deviates from a parabola
  • Lindeberg requires higher-order stencils — computationally expensive

Section 6 — Quantitative Results

50-Image Test Suite: Full Results

DetectorPrecisionRecallF1 ScoreOptimal Param
Roberts Cross0.4590.7630.556T = 40
Sobel0.4580.7640.556T = 40
Scharr0.4580.7630.556T = 45
Haar DWT0.4120.7610.499k = 2.5
Canny0.4580.5770.487σ = 0.1
Sub-Pixel (Poly.)0.5020.5280.476T = 0.5
Sub-Pixel (Lindeberg)0.5040.4410.434T = 0.5
Deriche0.3060.5730.388α = 0.8
LoG Zero-Crossings0.1830.9820.305σ = 0.7
Key finding Roberts (1963 forward difference, no smoothing) ties with Scharr (2000 Fourier-optimised) at F1 = 0.556. The right threshold matters more than the right filter on this dataset.
Future Direction For future work, to actually isolate the features better, we should use Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) rather than the simple ROI mask.
Implementation — github.com/rkhurmi/EdgeDetection_CSII

Code Architecture

detection/ module
Detector Implementations
  • first_order.py Roberts, Prewitt, Sobel, Scharr
  • second_order.py Marr–Hildreth LoG
  • canny.py Full 4-step Canny pipeline
  • deriche.py Recursive IIR detector
  • sub_pixel.py Polynomial + Lindeberg
  • wavelet.py Haar DWT detector
Root files
  • error.py EdgeEvaluation — P, R, F1, r=2px matching
  • threshold.py Batch eval + parameter sweeps
# threshold.py — parameter sweep (same pattern all 9 detectors)
threshold_values = [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100]
for T in threshold_values:
    for img_path, lbl_path in train_pairs:
        detected = FirstOrder(img_path, T).sobel()
        gt, poly  = label_txt_to_outline_mask(lbl_path, shape)
        ev = EdgeEvaluation(detected, gt, polygon_px=poly)
        scores.append(ev.calculate_f1_score())
# Select T with max mean F1 on training subset
optimal_T = threshold_values[np.argmax(mean_f1_per_threshold)]

# YOLO polygon → binary outline mask
def label_txt_to_outline_mask(txt_path, shape):
    polygon_px = np.stack([xy[:,0]*w, xy[:,1]*h], axis=1)
    cv2.polylines(mask, [polygon_px], isClosed=True,
                  color=255, thickness=2)
    return mask, polygon_px
